channel.title: NYT > Technology
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: More! More! More! Tech Workers Max Out Their A.I. Use.
item.description: At a number of companies, employees compete on leaderboards to show how much A.I. they’re using. They’re racking up big bills along the way.
item.dc:creator: Kevin Roose
item.media:credit: Erik Carter

item.title: Polymarket Says It Deals in Truth, but Its Social Feeds Are Filled With Falsehoods
item.description: A review of the betting market’s social media feeds found it has published hundreds of false and misleading posts.
item.dc:creator: Stuart A. Thompson, David Yaffe-Bellany and Mike Isaac
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: White House Unveils A.I. Policy Aimed at Blocking State Laws
item.description: The Trump administration on Friday released new guidelines for federal legislation on the technology, recommending some safeguards for children and consumer protections for energy costs.
item.dc:creator: Cecilia Kang
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump and David Sacks, the Trump administration’s A.I. and crypto czar, during a round-table event in the White House last year.

item.title: ‘A.I.-Washing’ Layoffs? + Why L.L.M.s Can’t Write Well + Tokenmaxxing
item.description: Companies are using A.I. as a reason for layoffs, but the truth may be more complex.
item.dc:creator: Kevin Roose, Casey Newton, Rachel Cohn, Whitney Jones, Vjeran Pavic, Katie McMurran, Dan Powell, Rowan Niemisto and Diane Wong
item.media:credit: Photo Illustration: The New York Times; Photo: iStock/Getty Images

item.title: Elon Musk Is Responsible for Some Twitter Investor Losses, Jury Finds
item.description: The verdict was a rare loss for Mr. Musk, who investors claimed had tried to drive down the share price of the social media company, now called X, to renegotiate his $44 billion offer.
item.dc:creator: Kate Conger and Natallie Rocha
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: Elon Musk arriving at the Phillip Burton Federal Building and U.S. Courthouse in San Francisco in March.

item.title: U.S. Says 3 Tied to Silicon Valley Server Maker Broke Export Laws
item.description: Prosecutors said the men, including a co-founder of the company, Super Micro, had diverted servers containing Nvidia A.I. chips to China.
item.dc:creator: Kalley Huang and Ana Swanson
item.media:credit: Hector Retamal/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Super Micro’s exhibit area at the Mobile World Congress in Shanghai in June.

item.title: Jeff Bezos in Talks to Raise $100 Billion Fund to Transform Companies With A.I.
item.description: The new fund would operate alongside the Amazon founder’s A.I. start-up, Project Prometheus.
item.dc:creator: Cade Metz and Karen Weise
item.media:credit: Jeenah Moon for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jeff Bezos’ new investment fund could be among the largest in the world.

item.title: Have You Turned to A.I. For Advice on a Romantic Relationship?
item.description: People are using generative A.I. chatbots for help on everything from starting to ending a relationship. Have you? How did it turn out?
item.dc:creator: Kashmir Hill
item.media:credit: Helen Orr for The New York Times

item.title: The Long Farewell to Mark Zuckerberg’s Metaverse
item.description: Meta announced changes that effectively leave Mr. Zuckerberg’s vision of an immersive digital world based in virtual reality only on life support.
item.dc:creator: Eli Tan and Mike Isaac
item.media:credit: Paul Windle

item.title: A.I. Bots Can Act as Personal Digital Assistants, but There Are Serious Risks
item.description: New A.I. bots can do more than just chat. They can edit files, send emails, book trips and cause trouble.
item.dc:creator: Cade Metz
item.media:credit: Manuel Orbegozo for The New York Times
item.media:description: A Vals AI employee uses Claude Code to debug failures in an agent trace at their office in San Francisco.

item.title: AI Replica of Val Kilmer to Appear in ‘As Deep as the Grave’
item.description: Kilmer’s estate collaborated with the filmmakers to make a digital likeness of the actor, who died of cancer last year.
item.dc:creator: Reggie Ugwu

item.title: Silicon Valley Musters Behind-the-Scenes Support for Anthropic
item.description: Tech companies have been reluctant to directly confront Trump administration officials over their contract feud with the A.I. start-up.
item.dc:creator: Mike Isaac
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Anthropic’s stance on how its artificial intelligence can be used in autonomous weapons and domestic surveillance infuriated officials at the Pentagon.

item.title: Google Sits Pretty as A.I. Rivals Compete for Pentagon Favor
item.description: The tech giant has been rebuilding its relationship with the Defense Department and is poised to benefit as it sidesteps competitors’ controversies.
item.dc:creator: Kate Conger and Julian E. Barnes
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Kelsey McClellan for The New York Times
item.media:description: Google, which swore off military work in 2018, is quietly rebuilding its relationship with the Defense Department.

item.title: How to Follow the Game, Even When You Can’t See It Live
item.description: When previous commitments keep you from catching your team on television, keep up with it using free apps that quietly deliver scores and more to your phone.
item.dc:creator: J. D. Biersdorfer
item.media:credit: Apple
item.media:description: Google’s “Pin Live Score” feature in the “Dynamic Island” tab, on an iPhone 17 Pro Max.

item.title: Turing Award Goes to Inventors of Quantum Cryptography
item.description: In the 1980s, Charles Bennett and Gilles Brassard created a new kind of encryption that would be impregnable.
item.dc:creator: Cade Metz
item.media:credit: Lise Raymond, via Association for Computing Machinery
item.media:description: On Wednesday, the Association for Computing Machinery said Charles Bennett, left, and Gilles Brassard had won this year’s Turing Award, often called the Nobel Prize of computing.

item.title: Silicon Valley Bet on War. The Bets Are Paying Off.
item.description: After years of criticism and financial risk, Palantir, Anthropic and small start-ups are generating rewards from their investments in defense tech.
item.dc:creator: Sheera Frenkel
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Photo illustration by Max-o-Matic

item.title: U.S. Says Anthropic Is an ‘Unacceptable’ National Security Risk
item.description: In a legal filing, the government said it questioned whether the A.I. start-up could be a “trusted partner” in wartime, which led it to label the company a supply chain risk.
item.dc:creator: Sheera Frenkel
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Anthropic has accused the Pentagon of labeling it a “supply chain risk” to punish it on ideological grounds.

item.title: Arizona Files Criminal Charges Against Kalshi, the Prediction Site
item.description: The state accused Kalshi of operating an illegal gambling business, escalating the legal battle between states and prediction markets.
item.dc:creator: Natallie Rocha and David Yaffe-Bellany
item.media:credit: Rebecca Noble/Reuters
item.media:description: Kris Mayes, the Arizona attorney general. On Tuesday, she filed criminal charges against Kalshi.

item.title: China Ramps Up Scrutiny of Meta’s Acquisition of Manus
item.description: The country appears to be cracking down on people linked to the acquisition of Manus, a Singapore company with Chinese roots, as President Trump prepares to visit Beijing.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson, Meaghan Tobin, Paul Mozur and Eli Tan
item.media:credit: Alex Plavevski/EPA, via Shutterstock
item.media:description: Meta announced in December that it had bought Manus, a Singapore artificial intelligence start-up that has Chinese roots.

item.title: Netanyahu Posts ‘Proof of Life’ Video as A.I. Sows Doubts About What’s Real
item.description: The unusual video is the latest demonstration that artificial intelligence is undermining trust — even in footage that is authentic.
item.dc:creator: Stuart A. Thompson and Tiffany Hsu
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Trapped in a Self-Driving Car During an Anti-Robot Attack
item.description: In San Francisco, some passengers of autonomous taxis have experienced an unexpected hazard: being stuck in the vehicles when the cars are assaulted.
item.dc:creator: Erin Griffith
item.media:credit: Loren Elliott for The New York Times
item.media:description: Self-driving cars have introduced novel and sometimes worrisome quirks to car travel.

item.title: Nvidia Debuts New A.I. Product at GTC Developer Conference
item.description: At the opening of the company’s annual conference, Jensen Huang leaned on technology from a recent deal to show how artificial intelligence is changing.
item.dc:creator: Kalley Huang and Tripp Mickle
item.media:credit: Manuel Orbegozo for The New York Times
item.media:description: Nvidia chief executive Jensen Huang at the company’s GTC conference in San Jose on Monday.

item.title: How Trump Drove a Wedge Between Florida Republicans Over A.I.
item.description: A Florida bill that would have regulated artificial intelligence, backed by Gov. Ron DeSantis, failed to gain traction after President Trump made it clear he did not want states to rein in the technology.
item.dc:creator: David McCabe and Patricia Mazzei
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gov. Ron DeSantis of Florida proposed a package of rules he called an A.I. “bill of rights.”
